coredata.dc:title: Recent trends and emerging strategies for aptasensing technologies for illicit drugs detection
coredata.dc:creator: Soni, Shringika
Jain, Utkarsh
Burke, Donald H.
Chauhan, Nidhi
coredata.dc:description: Aptamers are ligand-binding oligonucleotide sequences with high affinities and selectivities towards their targets. Concomitantly, their excellent recognition property has led to their being adapted in analytical applications, drug, diagnostics, and many other platforms in the area of forensic science, and in many respects, they are superior to analogous antibody-based assays. Recently, major progress has been made in biosensing applications of aptamers that offer the possibility of rapid and on-spot detection of seized drugs or illegal substances. Furthermore, aptamers may facilitate a future advancement in targeted delivery of anti-narcotics in the progression towards therapy. Hence, this review article provides an overview of electrochemical, optical, and colorimetric aptasensors developed so far and their potential future role in narcotics detection. We review advances made in aptamer sensing technology for cocaine, methamphetamine, codeine, and other opioid screening, and the potential obstacle at every stage of aptasensor development is also discussed. Finally, innovative concepts of using advanced nanomaterials and different sensing platforms are proposed for new generation aptasensor development for recreational drugs detection.

head.ce:title: Recent trends and emerging strategies for aptasensing technologies for illicit drugs detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Aptamers are ligand-binding oligonucleotide sequences with high affinities and selectivities towards their targets. Concomitantly, their excellent recognition property has led to their being adapted in analytical applications, drug, diagnostics, and many other platforms in the area of forensic science, and in many respects, they are superior to analogous antibody-based assays. Recently, major progress has been made in biosensing applications of aptamers that offer the possibility of rapid and on-spot detection of seized drugs or illegal substances. Furthermore, aptamers may facilitate a future advancement in targeted delivery of anti-narcotics in the progression towards therapy. Hence, this review article provides an overview of electrochemical, optical, and colorimetric aptasensors developed so far and their potential future role in narcotics detection. We review advances made in aptamer sensing technology for cocaine, methamphetamine, codeine, and other opioid screening, and the potential obstacle at every stage of aptasensor development is also discussed. Finally, innovative concepts of using advanced nanomaterials and different sensing platforms are proposed for new generation aptasensor development for recreational drugs detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Addiction to recreational and illicit drugs is a consequential healthcare problem for the 21st Century across the globe. Based on worldwide comparative data from the World Health Organization (WHO) for the years 2000 and 2016 [1], the total number of drug abusers increased 26.7%, along with an increase of 47.4% in the mortality rate. According to World Drug Report, 2017 by the United Nations, ∼4.2% of the population of the United Kingdom is having drug use disorder (DUDs) or facing drug addiction [2], in which 140 million population of 15–64 years are addicted to cannabis or its derivatives, whereas 0.6%-0.8% of the global population were reported to consume heroin, morphine, and nonprescription opioids [3,4]. A nationwide survey between the years 2017–2018 in India demonstrated that approximately 1.14% of the population uses heroin, whereas 0.96% and 0.52% were addicted to pharmaceutical opioids and opium, respectively [5]. In addition, cocaine, amphetamine-type stimulants, and hallucinogens have use prevalences of 0.10%, 0.18%, and 0.12%, respectively, in the Indian population. Recreational use, drug overdoses, and addictions cause behavioral and psychological changes in users with symptomatic analgesia, sedation, depression, and hyperactivity, and they sometimes lead to hypoxia and ultimately death [6,7].

Accurate detection and rapid monitoring of drug abuse are of great importance in the field of forensic science and criminology. Initial hours of drug detection in biological samples are critical due to the rapid pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics (PK-PD) of the drugs [8,9]. In this regard, point-of-care (POC) detection kits for on-spot drug detection are becoming a center of attraction among forensic analysts and law enforcement personnels. Conventionally, recreational and illicit drugs are detected by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), mass spectroscopy (MS), and many other analytical assays [10]. However, these conventional methods are expensive, time-consuming, and require standardized analytical laboratories with expertise in equipment handling and data analysis. Thus, the development of cost-effective, rapid, selective, and real-time drug detection has remained a compelling goal to scientific communities.

In recent years, several electrochemical, optical, and wearable sensors have been developed to detect recreational drugs in biological samples including detection of cocaine [11,12], 3,4-methylenedioxy​methamphetamine (MDMA) [13–15], lysergic acid diethylamide (LSD) [16,17], morphine [18–20], and marijuana [21–23]. These sensors become preferable due to higher selectivity, minimal cross-reactivity in most cases, low cost, portability, and miniaturization possibility [24]. OPI-MOR-HER Drug Test, immunochromatographic test and Fen-Her™, DABIT™, NarcoCheck® ID-Test® are colorimetric on-spot kits that are available commercially for recreational drug abuse detection [25–27]. Nevertheless, immunoassays or immunosensors and enzyme-based detection platforms have limitations in terms of stability, cross-reactivity, and size of the analytical target [28–30]; therefore, scientific communities need to focus on developing new sensing platforms with higher sensitivity, selectivity, and stability for recreational drug detection [10,31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Roles of aptamers in sensing technology
ce:section.ce:para:
Aptamers are oligonucleotides (typically 15–40 base pair (bp) long) with 3D structures that confer excellent recognition features, and they are typically smaller than antibodies or enzymes as biorecognition molecules. The word aptamer was coined from the Latin word ‘Aptus’ meaning ‘to fit,’ and this lock-and-key concept between the aptamer and target molecules can be achieved by in vitro selection and evolution process in Systematic Evolution of Ligands by EXponential enrichment (SELEX) [32–34], as illustrated in Fig. 1 . The SELEX method includes the selection of target binding sequences from a library of 1014-1016 random sequence oligonucleotides via repetitive amplification and binding analysis to generate highly selective aptamer sequences [35–38]. Aptamers also exhibit excellent dissociation constants (Kd) with their associated target molecules in the range from pico- to nano- to micro-molar levels [36,39]. In addition, aptamers exhibit several competitive advantages over antibodies, such as:
(i)
Having wider ranges of the greater chemical diversity of binding targets.
(ii)
Being more amenable to chemical or nanomaterial tagging.
(iii)
Being easily obtained from synthetic chemicals.
(iv)
Most importantly low molecular weight and excellent stability during repetitive denaturation and renaturation.

Aptamers can form complex structures such as three-way junctions or G-quadruplex tertiary structures, and they can be subjected to polymerase/endonucleases mediated reactions for target detection in several applications. The three-way junction is a very basic structural format of aptamers that can be formed by a single folded chain or by two or more annealed strands. This format of aptamer provides a moderately hydrophobic binding pocket at the central branching point and toehold domain on each arm, making the design more flexible for a reusable sensing platform. Additionally, they are also considered as artificial chemical entities with the recognition properties of antibodies but with significantly higher selectivity, negligible immunogenicity, effective reproducibility, and improved stability [40–42]. In summary, these characters make aptamers ideal candidates as biorecognition templates for biosensors.

For many applications, DNA aptamers are more useful in terms of designing reusable aptasensors, whereas RNA aptasensors are more often employed for single-use applications due to ribonucleases-based aptamer degradation [43], although this sensitivity can be overcome by chemical modification such as 2′F-substituted pyrimidines or by using enantiomeric aptamers known as Spiegelmers [44–46]. Development in this field has been growing since the first uses of optical [47] and electrochemical [48] aptasensors in the years 1996 and 2004, respectively. In the last decade, numerous aptasensors have been developed for the detection of proteins, microorganisms, pharmaceuticals, nucleic acids, and metal ions. They have shown excellent sensitivity and selectivity for the target molecules; therefore, forensic scientists are also trying to develop aptamer-based sensors for on-spot detection of drug abuse.

Narcotics drugs are the category of non-medical chemicals that are prohibited by health care workers and regulations of state administration because of risks to the users [49]. Drug abuse or addiction can cause adverse effects such as heart failure, anxiety, organ failure, hallucinations, and sometimes death, in addition to associated criminal activities [6,7]. Recently, the uses of marijuana, cocaine, methamphetamine, LSD, and MDMA have increased dramatically as party drugs among youths of age 15–40 [3,4,50]; therefore, these are frequently encountered in forensic drug analysis.

Narcotics screening and analysis are considered crucial areas among forensic scientists and medical professionals. Conventional chromatography and laboratory-based drug monitoring techniques are complex, time-consuming, require costly sophisticated instrumentation and expertise in sample preparation and result interpretation [10,51,52]. Hence the development of next-generation rapid, affordable, and highly selective on-spot detection kits is the uttermost priority in forensic science. Though several immunosensors have been reported for narcotics drug detection [53,54], antibody-based assays continue to be limited by stability and false-positive results.

The field of forensic science has gained profits from aptamer technology and great progress has been achieved in designing several aptasensors in analytical chemistry. By taking advantage of highly selective aptamers, the studies described below explained the mechanism of electrochemical, optical, and colorimetric aptasensors for sensitive detection of narcotics in biological specimens. Four trends that have been explored in aptamer-based sensing techniques in forensics are especially promising: 1) the use of split aptamers, 2) polymerases/endonuclease strand displacement strategies, 3) aptamer-tagged nanomaterials, and 4) dye-tagged aptamers. The principles of this categorized aptasensing are explained below and are briefly illustrated in Fig. 2 .

Furthermore, the following sections summarize the capabilities, advantages, and limitations of each of these approaches, as reflected in the current state of the field. The summary of recent trends in aptasensing technology for recreational and illicit drug detection is explained in Table 1 .

Some conventional aptamers exhibit a high degree of folding in the presence and sometimes in the absence of analyte that results in high background and lowers sensor sensitivity [55,56]. This limitation can be overcome by splitting the aptamer into two or three fragments that show higher stability in the presence of the target ligand with minimal misfolding. Split aptasensing has a higher selectivity and sensitivity due to the dual recognition mechanism for small molecules, which is absent in sandwich assays due to steric hindrance of the molecule to bind with two probes simultaneously [55]. Three different strategies of split aptasensing are especially notable: aptamer-target complex structure, hairpin loop structure of aptamer, and three-way junction architecture of aptamer (Fig. 3 ).

The aptamer-target complex strategy takes advantage of the fact that interactions with the target are generally through conserved regions in the aptamer, whereas non-conserved flanking loops outside the binding pocket can often be sliced without affecting the binding affinity of the aptamer-target complex. In some designs, even the conserved binding region can split into reporter and capture probes to develop a variety of sensors. Building upon this strategy, Abnous et al. used a split aptamer technology-based sensing strategy to enhance the sensitivity towards cocaine detection [57]. They tagged one strand of cocaine split aptamer with Black Hole Quencher-1 (BHQ-1) and labeled the other strand with both tetramethylrhodamine (TAMRA) quencher and fluorescein (FAM) fluorophore. A double fragment aptamer was formed in the presence of cocaine to induce proximity of TAMRA and SNP which amplified fluorescence intensity. At the same time, FAM-based fluorescent was quenched by both BHQ-1 and TAMRA, thus relative fluorescence intensity (TAMRAfluorescence intensity/ FAMfluorescence intensity) was increased in the presence of the target. This double quenching property of FAM improved the fluorescent aptasensor to detect at little as 84 pM of cocaine within 30 min. Another study on non-aggregated nanoparticles with gold cores and silver shells (Au@Ag) based colorimetric aptasensor for methamphetamine (METH) detection also exhibited excellent results [58]. They studied the Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR) signals for Au@Ag core–shell NP-conjugated reporter probe (RP) and magnetic bead-conjugated capture probes interaction with METH-aptamer in the presence and absence of target. The capture and partially-complementary reporter probe bound to METH-aptamer in the absence of target, forming a sandwich structure complex. When an external magnetic field was applied to the system, Au@Ag core–shell NPs induced SPR signals were reduced due to the effective removal of hybridized Au@Ag-DNA sandwich structure. However, the aptamer prefers to bind with METH instead of capture or receptor probes, thus freely available Au@Ag demonstrated higher SPR signals in presence of the analyte, and a visible color change from light yellow to deep yellow was observed, as illustrated in Fig. 4 . In the presence of METH, aptamer bound to target due to higher affinity compared to probes, and higher absorbance was reported in UV–Vis spectroscopy due to free Au@Ag NPs in solution (Fig. 4A). Fig. 4B demonstrates that SPR signals increased with increasing METH concentration from 0.5 nM to 200 nM with LOD of 0.1 nM. This increased absorbance explained deformities of Au@Ag-dsDNA-MBs complex with aptamer and the visible change in color from yellow to pale orange. A major advantage of using Au@Ag NPs over AuNPs alone was its stronger SPR signal intensity, which conferred better signal transduction property of Au@Ag NP.

The second strategy of split aptasensing exploits loop regions of partially complementary domains in single-stranded oligonucleotides. These regions act both as binding sites for the target and as a split-site if the binding domain is in the stem region, though it increases the chance of misfolding and the error rate in target detection. In this direction, a study published in Analytical Chemistry exhibited a rapid label-free aptasensor development for cocaine determination by combining a target-specific aptamer and a peroxidase-mimicking DNAzyme (G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme) sequence and using it as a recognition template [59]. Hoögsteen hydrogen-bonded G-quadruplex has excellent property of ligand binding and stability under complex environments [60,61]. The authors of this study observed that the aptamer-cocaine interaction turned on the DNAzyme activity of G-quadruplex in the presence of hemin, and the catalytic reaction of Hydroquinone (HQ) reported a change in electrochemical signals. Although these aptamers were less sensitive than those described above (LOD of 1.0 μM of cocaine and a linear range of 1.0–500 μM), the detection was reported to be rapid with a response time of 9.0 min without any intermediate label.

The third strategy of split aptasensing exploits three-way junction structures of the aptamers as privileged structures to achieve specific morphologies for multivariant binding with specific targets. A novel ‘sandwich-type’ approach was adopted by Wen et al. in which they separated the cocaine aptamer into two strands – designated capture and reporter probe – to confer the properties of recognition and signal generation, respectively [62]. The capture probe was appended to a self-assembled, rigid DNA tetrahedron on the sensing platform, while a biotin-labeled reporter probe induced higher electrochemical signals in the presence of cocaine. Avidin-horseradish peroxidase (avidin-HRP) complex acted as redox probe and interacted with biotin tagged reporter probe in the presence of cocaine. This redox reaction coupled with TMB (3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine) and H2O2 interchangeable catalysis reduction for observable electrochemical peaks which increased with cocaine concentration (LOD 33 nM). In another study, an optical aptasensor with a ruthenium polypyridyl complex of Ru[(bpy)2(bqdppz)]2+ ligand was designed for selective cocaine detection [63]. This ligand interacted with an intermolecular G-quadruplex conformation of the cocaine aptamer to remain in an inactive ‘switch-off’ condition. Complex dissociation in the presence of as little as 5.0 nM cocaine caused a reduction of fluorescent signals. To further improve the method, Nie et al. developed a hybrid enzyme-linked aptamer assay (ELAA) for colorimetric detection of cocaine [64]. The ELAA based technique works on the principle of ELISA where one part of the cleaved cocaine aptamer is immobilized on a 96-well plate and forms a ternary complex in the presence of the other (biotinylated) aptamer fragment and cocaine. In the presence of streptavidin-HRP (SA-HRP), catalytic oxidation of TMB–H2O2 led to a color change and exhibited a good linear relationship in absorption from 5.0 to 200 μM cocaine. This colorimetric ELAA method exhibited a comparatively higher LOD of 2.8 μM, potentially because of higher aptamer density on the sensing platform. However, splitting the aptamer reduced its affinity for the target along with compromising the stability of the ternary aptamer-target complex and ultimately lowered the sensitivity of the system. This limitation can be overcome by the signal amplification approach by combining fluorophore, quencher, or nanomaterials with aptamer. But specific downstream biochemical reactions for the assembly of split aptamer fragments, limits combining signal amplification approaches [55]. Hence further improvements to this method are still needed to avoid compromising aptasensor sensitivity.

The target-induced strand displacement strategies have recently been introduced in sensor development and the application of nucleases/ polymerases enzyme also supported sensitivity improvement. Some examples of enzyme bases aptasensing technologies are explained in Fig. 5 .

Nuclease-assisted aptasensing: In this method, aptamer probe is allowed to hybridize with complementary anchor sequence, and this interaction breaks in the presence of target to form aptamer-target complex. Further incubation of exonuclease enzymes digests the anchor sequence, which gives relatively different signals from aptamer-anchor duplex oligonucleotides. Lu et al. prepared endonuclease-stimulated optical aptasensors [65]. They designed two subunits of a target-specific aptamer that contained 3′ and 5′ extensions with endonuclease sensitive complementary sequences. One aptameric subunit carried a fluorophore and assembled with the partially-complementary blocking strand that carried a quencher to block the formation of the aptamer complex. The blocking strand is displaced in the presence of cocaine as the full aptamer-analyte complex is formed, thereby activating fluorescence. An innovation of this design is that endonuclease digestion of the aptamer-analyte complex degrades the complex, releases the fluorophore, and regenerates free analyte cocaine, which can then bind another aptamer complex and catalytically drive further cycles of fluorophore activation. This autocatalytic release of fluorophores gave a time-dependent increase in fluorescence changes in the presence of at least 100 nM concentration of cocaine, with a linear range extending to 20 μM.

Polymerase-assisted aptasensing: In this technique, one end of the aptamer probe is partially complementary to an anchor oligonucleotide that binds to the aptamer-target complex. The anchor sequence amplifies in the presence of polymerase-induced extension and changes the recorded signal. For example, in a study published in Biosensors & Bioelectronics, a cocaine-binding aptamer was engineered to refold into an active, three-way junction structure in the presence of cocaine, which further hybridized with a complementary ‘anchor’ sequence. In the presence of Klenow fragment (KF) and ferrocene-modified UTP (Fc-dUTP), the anchor oligonucleotide was isothermally extended and an increase in conductivity was observed due to polymerization of Fc molecules and removal of cocaine from oligonucleotide complex. This gave immediate evidence for surface confinement of the Fc labels that exhibited facilitated electron transfer kinetics. Furthermore, an increase in conductivity in presence of cocaine also indicated target-induced polymerase extension and Fc-dUTP immobilization. They showed LOD of 97 nM and linear response over a wide range of cocaine (0.2–5.0 μM) [66]. The KF polymerases mediated aptasensing works on the following principle: When target binds with aptamer, the polymerase reaction between primers and nucleotide mixture was initiated and conformational transition of aptamer was observed to give significant detectable signals. Unlike polymerase chain reaction (PCR), this technique does not need to follow specific laboratory conditions and thermal cycling, thus supporting their use for inexpensive and robust biosensing. This same research team previously used a similar polymerase-based strand displacement strategy in developing a new fluorescence method for aptamer targeted cocaine detection down to 1.0 nM target concentration [67]. For that study, SYBR green I (SGI) intercalating dye was used instead of Fc-dUTP, as the SGI gave a fluorescent signal when ds-DNA completely formed on the template of hairpin aptamer, analogous to the use of this dye in quantitative PCR. In continuation, Qiu et al modified the above technique for better applicability of the sensing platform [68]. They used GO along with SGI dye due to the higher ability of these two agents to interact with ds-DNA, which was formed in this study during polymerase triggered isothermal circular strand-displacement amplification. Significant fluorescent enhancement was achieved in 190 nM concentration of cocaine. GO has the advantage of overcoming the SGI based high background signals and non-specific adsorption on the developed sensing platform.

Similarly, a simple, label-free ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor was designed on the basis of 2-amino-5,6,7-trimethyl-1,8-naphthyridine (ATMND) and SGI signaling [69]. As shown in Fig. 6 A, ATMND fortuitously binds in the same site as cocaine within the three-way junction of the cocaine aptamer. In the absence of a target, the flanking bases quenched fluorescence from the ATMND dye, while SGI gave signals continuously. In contrast, cocaine displaced ATMND and restored its fluorescence without disturbing SGI signaling in the presence of the analyte. With increasing cocaine concentration, the ATMND signal intensity also enhanced while the SGI signal was held steady (Fig. 6B), and the fluorescence ratio of the two dyes (IATMND/ISGI) was reported to be linear in the range of 0.1–10 μM of cocaine (Fig. 6C), enabling accurate detection in complex biofluids within 20 sec. Fig. 6D illustrates each step in this sensing application, in which curve (a) showed a strong peak at 412 nm corresponding to free ATMND only because free SGI has low fluorescence emission efficiency in the absence of DNA. By the addition of cocaine aptamer, the emission peak at 532 nm was decreased and corresponded directly to SGI only (curve b). This can be justified on the basis of stable entrapment of SGI via the intercalation and significant improvement in its fluorescence, whereas ATMND is continuously quenched at equilibrium in this three-way aptamer junction due to the presence of the moderately hydrophobic binding pocket of 2 cytosines and 1 thymine. The curve (c) demonstrated immediate fluorescence recovery of ATMND signal in presence of cocaine, without affecting SGI fluorescence emission. This supported cocaine-induced ATMND displacement without altering SGI and dsDNA interaction. Thus, this study confirmed the role of ATMND in fluorescence signaling in the presence of cocaine, whereas SGI exhibited good stability with dsDNA stems in the three-way junction of aptamer whether the target is present or absent.
In summary, strand displacement strategies have recently received considerable attention in sensing application, and they offer considerable promise for ultrasensitive sensor development.

With the rise of nanotechnology, several R&D laboratories are currently using advanced nanomaterials in sensing platforms. In particular, the dual optical and voltammetric properties of quantum dots (QDs) make them extremely valuable in aptasensor development. Zhang et al. used QDs as labels in cocaine-based aptasensors [70], in which a tagged aptamer initially reacted with cocaine and simultaneously annealed to a secondary binding aptamer conjugated to CdS QDs to form a 3D structural complex. Acid-induced dissolution of QDs exhibited higher voltammetric signals in a wide target linear range of 0.15 μM-2.0 mM and reported a LOD of 50 μM in human serum samples. More recently, Shahdost-fard and Roushani developed AgNP-tagged aptamers on an electrochemical sensor modified with CdTe QDs to detect cocaine. The immobilized tagged aptamer showed conformational changes to form a three-way junction and enhanced steric hindrance in the presence of the target analyte [71]. This design of application of double QDs takes advantage of conformational restriction of immobilized aptamers and repulsion between the negative charges of phosphate group with AgNPs at aptamer to prohibit electron transfer on the electrode surface. The higher sensitivity of CdTe QDs and selectivity of aptamer contributed to a reported LOD of 5.0 ± 0.1 pM and a linear range of 50–450 pM cocaine. Further improvements in nanomaterial-tagged aptamers may be achieved by focusing on optimization of spacer arms, varying the immobilization density of nanomaterial tagged aptamers, and ensuring a suitable orientation of the aptamers relative to the nanoparticle surface. In this direction, a study published in Microchimica Acta used cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH) nanosheets to quench the fluorescent property of carbon dots (CDs), conjugated on aptamers for METH detection [72]. The distance between CoOOH nanosheets and CDs was reported to be 7.6 Å, and the overlapping absorbance spectrum confirmed the possibility of energy transfer between nanosheets and QDs via the FRET mechanism. They explained the possible interactions of CoOOH nanosheets, CDs, aptamers, and METH through molecular docking. It justified the high specific areas, electron transfer rate, and excellent optical absorption of CoOOH nanosheets for exhibiting quenching mechanisms. The nucleic acid bases of CDs-conjugated aptamers in this study bound to CoOOH nanosheets via van-der Waal force and quenching occurred. These weak bonds broke in the presence of METH to form aptamer-target complex and restored CD fluorescence, which increased with increasing concentration in a range of 5–156 nM. This study opened new doors for the application of oxyhydroxide-2D nanomaterials and QDs based FRET mechanisms in aptasensing technologies, as oxyhydroxide-based nanomaterials are easy to synthesize and can exhibit excellent optical and electronic properties [73,74].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanomaterials fabricated aptasensing in narcotics detection
ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials fabricated electrochemical and optical sensors development has grown rapidly in recent years [30,75,76]. However, aptamer-based nano-sensing technologies are still under development and are very limited for monitoring illicit drugs. Initial studies using dye-tagged aptamers were done by Baker et al., who developed a ‘signal on’ electrochemical aptasensor that exhibited significant redox potential due to reduction of methylene blue (MB) in the presence of cocaine [77]. The MB-tagged aptamers folded in the three-way junction in the presence of cocaine and promoted e- transfer to detect as little as 10 μM target molecule within seconds. To further improve the cocaine sensing method, Zhang et al. [78] developed a ferrocene (Fc)-labeled aptasensor instead of MB as used by Baker et al, because MB may intercalate into nucleic acid duplexes [79]. Using Fc as a redox label, they regenerated cathodic peak current in the presence of cocaine within 5 min and reported a LOD of 0.5 μM in buffer solution. To further improve aptamer immobilization on the sensing platform, Roushani and Shahdost-fard developed AuNP-tagged aptamers as recognition components on a multi-wall carbon nanotube (MWCNT)-modified sensing platform [80]. The aptamer remained partially unfolded in the absence of cocaine and allowed higher electron transfer, but this aptamer converted into a three-way junction in the presence of the target analyte. Hence, negatively charged AuNPs at the end of the aptamer came near to the modified electrode surface and inhibited electron transfer to reduce peak current, which gradually decreased with increasing cocaine concentration. These authors later incorporated platinum nanoparticles (PtNPs) to accelerate e- transfer kinetics for reduction of the redox probe, rutin, and they reported a LOD of 100 pM in the linear range of 1.0 nM − 11 μM [81]. In contrast, Mohammadi et al. reported a nanoporous gold (NPG) modified electrode cocaine aptasensor in which an aptamer that was conjugated with a 2,5-dihydroxybenzoic acid (DHBA) redox probe converted into a closed conformation in the presence of cocaine and reduced the distance between DHBA and electrode surface to enhance e- transfer efficiency [82]. Although these redox-reaction-based sensing applications are becoming popular, further advances are possible by exploring several parameters that affect the target analysis process. For instance, different redox states and pH conditions of each step in target sensing affects the activity of the tagged probe. In addition, the low concentration and short lifetime of the end product of the redox reaction is also challenging in analytical chemistry. Furthermore, the redox components have fixed reduction potential (E°); therefore, a range of reagents will be required during detection process.

The commercialization of aptasensors in narcotics detection has lagged behind the academic output, due in part to technical barriers and long, sensitive processes of aptamer discovery. Modifications in aptasensing technology need to meet the challenges of designing narcotics aptamers that should be highly selective and sensitive towards individual drugs or mixtures of drugs in any biological specimens or even those present on the surfaces of clothing, currency, or other materials. In addition, it is very difficult to enrich low concentrations or combinations of recreational and illicit drugs in biological samples. Therefore selective aptasensors may continue to be developed for on-spot forensic applications [83–85]. Newly-developed aptamers will need to retain selectivity while undergoing conformational changes upon narcotics binding, while also avoiding agglomeration or resistance on electrode surfaces to maintain the higher electrochemically-active surface area. Although emerging nanotechnology aspects in aptamer-based sensing seem easy, size-controlled nanomaterial synthesis and label-free binding of nanomaterials to aptamers remain a challenge. Therefore, signal amplification in aptasensors is recommended through more use of endonucleases or other enzymes (such as horseradish peroxidase, HRP), aptamer splitting in displacement strategies [62,86,87], or QD-tagged aptamers. On the other hand, traditional metallic QDs, such as CdS, CdTe, or CuInS2, exhibit toxicity towards oligonucleotides and make recovery difficult [88–90]. Therefore, QDs developed by green synthesis, (such as carbon dots, CDs) may be used as fluorescence probes due to their non-toxic nature, cost-effectiveness, and wide availability [91,92].

Additionally, recent studies on nanomaterials-based aptasensing technologies also bring to view that exploitation of advanced nanomaterials, such as perovskites, upconversion nanoparticles, and their hybrids can be utilized as excellent transducers [93,94]. The large surface areas of these materials will also improve aptamer immobilization for reliable analytical determination [95,96]. Furthermore, appropriate integration of nanomaterials and aptamers for multiplex detection of targets in facile and cost-effective setup for on-spot detection is another area that needs to be explored [97–99].

Recently several interdisciplinary approaches have helped in next-generation aptamer synthesis and developing new aptasensors. To accelerate aptamer selection and achieve higher selectivity, recent innovations include electrode-bound cDNA or nanomaterials in Capture SELEX [100,101] or graphene-oxide-bound libraries in GO-SELEX [102,103]. These approaches avoid ligand immobilization, which can alter its recognition by affinity probes, and they encourage recognition of the appropriate chemical moieties of the target molecule in solution, thereby reducing the time required to engineer sensors from newly-selected aptamers [104]. To accelerate commercialization and the use of aptamer sensing in on-spot applications, aptamers are also proposed to be incorporated into paper-based devices, portable SPR, lateral flow assays, and smartphones. Additionally, smartphone applications are particularly attractive for quick, on-spot detection of drug misuse. Other than these strategies, enzyme-linked oligonucleotide assay (ELONA) based aptasensing technology may improve dual detection of recreational and illicit drugs where aptamers will work as capture molecules and antibodies or secondary aptamers act as reporter molecules [105]. We also speculate that hybrid diagnostic systems such as microfluidics or molecular beacons will help to eventuate the efficient development of aptasensor end products. It can be assumed that the integration of distinct areas of electronics and nanotechnology with aptasensing might have excellent potential to revolutionize all reported options for narcotics detection in forensic science.

The past few decades have exhibited a surge of next-generation aptamer-based biosensors development in narcotics drugs detection. Multiple strategies have been used and their application in narcotics detection demonstrated great potential in forensic drug analysis, such as split aptamer technology [62,86,88], fluorophore quenching [106,107], and nanomaterials tagging as indicators [108,109]. Aptamers have several clear advantages relative to other classes of affinity probes in terms of flexibility in designing variations that are suitable for highly diverse detection platforms, diversity in target binding, and high selectivity. This review article has provided a summary of state-of-the-art developments in efficient aptasensors for on-spot narcotics detection and potential theranostic approaches over the last two decades. The article has described modified aptamer technology-assisted sensing mechanism and potential applications, such as the use of quantum dots in aptamer tagging or as immobilization substrates for oligonucleotides [108], DNA tetrahedron assembly [62], ELAA method [69], fluorophore quenching [106] or polymerase/endonuclease-assisted drug detection. Moreover, progress in aptamers-based biosensing systems for cocaine, methamphetamine, codeine, and opioid detection have been described. These discussions can be beneficial in future scientific research to develop more advanced aptasensors and improve forensic analysis technologies.

Shringika Soni: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Investigation. Utkarsh Jain: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Donald H. Burke: Supervision. Nidhi Chauhan: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.
